story:
You did not enter 239. That was the rule. The Minister and his family only. Even if you were on duty and invited in, you refused. The White Sentinel paced outside 239 South Fountain Street and listened.

The city was quiet because it had to be. West of Douglas Avenue, pressure built: cramped lives, bad air, quick tempers. Old Town sent up sparks of hope, and the Minister’s men put them out. He had moved his family within earshot on purpose—far from the powerful, close to the threat. His family hated it. His eldest son hated it most.

Owen complained about the smell, the noise, the closeness. He felt small among his rich school friends in East City, but he knew what his father’s power kept at bay. Without it, he thought, the Towners would storm their lawns and ruin everything. He hated Old Town mostly because he had to live beside it. Hear it. Its yelling. Its screaming. Its dying.

A flare rose from Warren Square. A cheer lifted and fell. Owen snapped his window shut, sat in his thin white chair, turned up his music, and tried to type. The first FUMP, FUMP, FUMP of gas canisters rolled across the night. Old tools still worked after the grid collapsed, and the Minister had stockpiled them. He had traded wheat for guns. Built a wall. Kept order by force so his city wouldn’t disappear like the others. Better, he told himself, to die at home than at the hands of marauders.

Another round. Rifle pops. Red emergency lights blinked on; Owen’s screen died. He sighed and headed down the stairs. They always ran toward his house, and they never got close. Then glass broke.

The Minister shoved him hard against the banister and dove. A man had come through the window, wrist cut, a flaming bottle dropping from his hand. Fire leapt. The intruder rolled, screaming. The Minister burned and did not scream. He went for the man’s throat. Fire ate cloth and skin. The intruder stopped moving. The Minister smothered himself on the rug, stood by will alone, and looked at his son. A ruined face. White eyes in blackened flesh. Owen collapsed.

More glass. A shot from the kitchen. A grunt. Another man came around the corner. Owen ran upstairs.

Outside, the White Sentinel sniffed. Fresh death. Fire. And a thump inside the house—purposeful, hunting. Orders said do not enter 239.

Do not go into the house.  
No.  
Not into the house.  
No.  
You may not enter 239.

He went in anyway.

The Minister lay dead. Rustling upstairs. The Sentinel moved toward the sound he trusted: the boy’s voice.

“Please. No. Help!”

He bounded up. The man on the landing had a pistol leveled. His voice shook with rage and grief: privilege, rationing, a son starved, an eye for an eye. He raised the gun at Owen’s head.

The Sentinel hit him. The pistol flew, skidding to the boy. The man drew a second gun from his boot and aimed at Owen. The angle was wrong. The Sentinel did what he was made to do. He lunged.

Two shots. One grazed Owen. One struck the Sentinel. He fell at Owen’s feet, lungs heaving, eyes wide. Owen gripped the fallen pistol and fired until it clicked dry. The last shot was at the man’s face.

Then Owen crawled back. He climbed onto the White Sentinel and held him. Warm blood seeped into his own. The Sentinel did not expect it, but was glad. The boy did not leave. When the Sentinel finally went still, they were bound together, arms and blood fused.

Owen never forgot. When he became Minister of Peace, he remembered the father who saved him—and the White Sentinel who made him.
